title: fix(personhog-replica): rm test before acquire
author: nickbest-ph

evidence.lived_experience: Every `pool.acquire()` call sends a health-check ping... The ~5ms baseline *is* that ping roundtrip through PgBouncer. The 30ms+ spikes are variance... confirmed by pool saturation sitting near 0% and connection churn being flat
evidence.organizational_memory: PgBouncer runs in transaction mode | personhog-router already has retry logic (max 3 retries with backoff) | `is_transient_error` in `common/database` | metric name `personhog_replica_db_pool_acquire_duration_ms`
evidence.uncertainty: Will monitor personhog-replica post deploy to see if we get the perf gains
evidence.causal_reasoning: Since PgBouncer runs in transaction mode, it reassigns backend Postgres connections between transactions anyway — the client connection (sqlx → PgBouncer) stays alive regardless. The ping isn't protecting against stale connections, it's just adding latency.

title: fix(test): dismiss Monaco autocomplete in HogQL E2E test
author: app/mendral-app

evidence.lived_experience: The test has been flaky since it was first added (~Feb 23), appearing as a failure in 6+ distinct runs in the last 7 days on master.
evidence.organizational_memory: none found
evidence.uncertainty: Not currently causing failures since the editor starts empty, but fixes a latent bug.
evidence.causal_reasoning: Monaco's HogQL autocomplete provider has space as a trigger character (`triggerCharacters: [' ', ',', '.', '{']` in `hogQLAutocompleteProvider.ts:89`) with a 300ms delay (`quickSuggestionsDelay: 300` in `QueryPane.tsx:67`). When `keyboard.type()` sends a space, Monaco starts a timer to show autocomplete suggestions. If the suggestion widget appears while subsequent characters are being typed, it can intercept keystrokes — corrupting the query.

title: fix(toolbar): handle percent-encoded hash and preserve URL fragment in OAuth redirect
author: fercgomes

evidence.lived_experience: The toolbar OAuth redirect flow (introduced in #49579) fails on production because browsers and proxies percent-encode the `:` and `,` delimiters in the URL hash fragment
evidence.organizational_memory: References PR #49579 as prior context, but no explicit team discussions or org-specific knowledge signals
evidence.causal_reasoning: browsers and proxies percent-encode the `:` and `,` delimiters in the URL hash fragment (`%3A` and `%2C`). The frontend regex that extracts the authorization code never matches, so the code exchange silently fails and the toolbar params stay in the URL. Additionally, the backend callback discards any original URL fragment... breaking hash-based routing for customers.

title: fix(web-vitals): update web vitals key to fix props update bug
author: jabahamondes

evidence.lived_experience: For example, switching from INP to CLS keeps showing `duration_ms` formatting instead of `numeric`, and goal line values stay at INP's thresholds (200/500) instead of updating to CLS's (0.1/0.25).
evidence.organizational_memory: insightDataLogic doesn't have a BindLogic in InsightViz.tsx, so its props never update after the initial mount. The propsQuery selector (which reads from (_, props) => props) stays stale. Since insightVizDataLogic.querySource reads from insightDataLogic.query, the trendsFilter remains frozen.
evidence.causal_reasoning: Root cause analysis traces stale propsQuery through selector chain to explain why trend filter stays frozen. Decision rationale: chose single-line key approach to avoid risk of modifying shared components (insightDataLogic, InsightViz) used across entire insight system.

title: fix: return empty 204 from Vercel proxy instead of body with {}
author: MattBro

evidence.lived_experience: EU Vercel installations were failing with 'An error occurred while creating the Account' despite our API returning 204 success responses.
evidence.organizational_memory: none found
evidence.causal_reasoning: The proxy in VercelRegionProxyMixin was wrapping 204 No Content responses from the EU region with Response(data={}, status=204). Since {} is truthy in Python, dispatch then serialized it into HttpResponse(content="{}", status=204, content_type="application/json") — violating the HTTP spec (204 must have no body) and causing Vercel to reject the response.

title: fix(ux): offramp modal fix and nits
author: adamleithp
error:
Command '['claude', '--model', 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001', '-p', 'You are analyzing a GitHub PR description or issue. Score it on TWO axes:
(A) SPEC QUALITY — how well does it describe what to build?
(B) HUMAN ENGAGEMENT — was a human genuinely thinking, or is this AI-generated boilerplate?

For every numeric field, score 0-100.

## A. Spec Quality Dimensions

1. outcome_clarity: Is the desired end state clear? Could two devs build the same thing?
2. error_states: Are error conditions, edge cases, failure modes described? 0 if none mentioned.
3. scope_boundaries: Clear what's IN and NOT in scope?
4. acceptance_criteria: Testable success conditions? Given/When/Then?
5. data_contracts: Data shapes, API contracts, schema changes? N/A if not data-related.
6. dependency_context: References to existing code, modules, APIs this touches?
7. behavioral_specificity: Concrete details (numbers, thresholds) vs vague ("should be fast")?
8. change_type: "feature"|"bugfix"|"refactor"|"dependency"|"docs"|"config"|"test"|"other"
9. spec_length_signal: "empty"|"minimal"|"short"|"medium"|"detailed"

## B. Human Engagement Signals

10. lived_experience: Author narrates what happened TO THEM? ("I ran into..." vs "The issue is...")
11. organizational_memory: References org knowledge an AI couldn't know? ("Per discussion with auth team")
12. uncertainty: Acknowledges unknowns? ("Should we also handle...?") AI never doubts itself.
13. negative_scope: Explicitly says what's NOT being done, with reasoning?
14. causal_reasoning: Explains WHY grounded in specific system context? (not "best practice")
15. genuine_edge_cases: System-specific edge cases, or generic CS concepts?
16. template_filler: (INVERTED: high = MORE template = bad) Is this just restating the diff?
17. overall_human_engagement: Overall: 80-100 clearly human, 50-79 mixed, 20-49 mostly AI, 0-19 pure AI.

## C. Task Precision

18. precision_required (0-100): How narrow is the correctness window for this task?
- HIGH (80-100): One correct implementation, many wrong ones. Payment logic, crypto, auth, race conditions, data migrations, protocol compliance, correctness-critical algorithms. Getting it 95% right means it's wrong.
- MEDIUM (40-79): Several valid approaches but meaningful constraints. API design, database schema, error handling strategy, performance optimization with specific targets.
- LOW (0-39): Many acceptable outputs. UI styling, documentation, boilerplate scaffolding, config changes, dependency bumps, adding a standard CRUD endpoint. "Good enough" is the bar.
- Key question: If you gave this task to 10 competent developers independently, how similar would their implementations be? High similarity = high precision required.

## D. Cognitive Questions

19. cognitive_questions (0-100): Does the author ask GENUINE questions showing they are thinking?
NOT template headers ("### What changed?"). NOT rhetorical. NOT checklists.
Look for: "Should we also handle X?", "I'm not sure if this affects Y", "Does anyone know if Z is still used?", "Would it be better to...?", "What happens when...?"
These signal the author is cognitively engaged — they're thinking about edge cases, seeking feedback, or expressing uncertainty about their own work.
- 80-100: Multiple genuine questions showing deep engagement with the problem
- 40-79: One or two real questions mixed with template
- 10-39: Template questions only, or questions that are really statements
- 0-9: No questions at all, or only boilerplate

## E. Testability of Success Criteria

20. testability (0-100): Could you write a FAILING test from this description alone, without reading the code?
This is NOT the same as acceptance_criteria. acceptance_criteria measures whether criteria exist. Testability measures whether you could AUTOMATE verification without reading the implementation.

Score by asking: "If I handed this to a QA engineer who has never seen the codebase, could they write a test that fails before the change and passes after?"

Examples to calibrate:

TESTABILITY 90, acceptance_criteria 85:
"POST /webhooks/stripe with valid HMAC-SHA256 signature returns 200. Invalid signature returns 401. Missing signature header returns 400."
→ QA engineer writes 3 HTTP tests immediately. No code reading needed.

TESTABILITY 20, acceptance_criteria 75:
"Given a user, when they log in, then they should be authenticated. Given invalid credentials, then login should fail gracefully."
→ Sounds testable but ISN'T. What endpoint? What response code? What does "gracefully" mean? 400? 401? 403? Redirect? QA engineer has to read the code or ask questions.

TESTABILITY 10, acceptance_criteria 60:
"This PR improves error handling across the auth module. Errors are now caught and reported consistently."
→ What errors? What does "consistently" mean? What's the observable difference? Cannot write any test.

TESTABILITY 5, acceptance_criteria 10:
"Refactors the middleware to use the new pattern. No functional changes."
→ Only testable assertion is "it still compiles." No behavioral test possible.

TESTABILITY 85, acceptance_criteria 40:
"Requests exceeding 100/minute per API key get 429 with Retry-After header set to seconds until reset."
→ Low acceptance_criteria (only one criterion) but HIGH testability (exact numbers, exact header, exact response code). One test, but it's completely automatable.

## Output
Return ONLY valid JSON, no markdown:
{
  "outcome_clarity": N, "error_states": N, "scope_boundaries": N,
  "acceptance_criteria": N, "data_contracts": N, "dependency_context": N,
  "behavioral_specificity": N,
  "change_type": "...", "spec_length_signal": "...",
  "lived_experience": N, "organizational_memory": N, "uncertainty": N,
  "negative_scope": N, "causal_reasoning": N, "genuine_edge_cases": N,
  "template_filler": N, "overall_human_engagement": N,
  "precision_required": N, "cognitive_questions": N, "testability": N,
  "evidence": {
    "lived_experience": "quote or none found",
    "organizational_memory": "quote or none found",
    "uncertainty": "quote or none found",
    "causal_reasoning": "quote or none found",
    "cognitive_questions": "quote the question or none found"
  },
  "classification": "human|ai_generated|mixed"
}

title: chore(endpoints): track endpoint execution
author: sakce
error:
Command '['claude', '--model', 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001', '-p', 'You are analyzing a GitHub PR description or issue. Score it on TWO axes:
(A) SPEC QUALITY — how well does it describe what to build?
(B) HUMAN ENGAGEMENT — was a human genuinely thinking, or is this AI-generated boilerplate?

For every numeric field, score 0-100.

## A. Spec Quality Dimensions

1. outcome_clarity: Is the desired end state clear? Could two devs build the same thing?
2. error_states: Are error conditions, edge cases, failure modes described? 0 if none mentioned.
3. scope_boundaries: Clear what's IN and NOT in scope?
4. acceptance_criteria: Testable success conditions? Given/When/Then?
5. data_contracts: Data shapes, API contracts, schema changes? N/A if not data-related.
6. dependency_context: References to existing code, modules, APIs this touches?
7. behavioral_specificity: Concrete details (numbers, thresholds) vs vague ("should be fast")?
8. change_type: "feature"|"bugfix"|"refactor"|"dependency"|"docs"|"config"|"test"|"other"
9. spec_length_signal: "empty"|"minimal"|"short"|"medium"|"detailed"

## B. Human Engagement Signals

10. lived_experience: Author narrates what happened TO THEM? ("I ran into..." vs "The issue is...")
11. organizational_memory: References org knowledge an AI couldn't know? ("Per discussion with auth team")
12. uncertainty: Acknowledges unknowns? ("Should we also handle...?") AI never doubts itself.
13. negative_scope: Explicitly says what's NOT being done, with reasoning?
14. causal_reasoning: Explains WHY grounded in specific system context? (not "best practice")
15. genuine_edge_cases: System-specific edge cases, or generic CS concepts?
16. template_filler: (INVERTED: high = MORE template = bad) Is this just restating the diff?
17. overall_human_engagement: Overall: 80-100 clearly human, 50-79 mixed, 20-49 mostly AI, 0-19 pure AI.

## C. Task Precision

18. precision_required (0-100): How narrow is the correctness window for this task?
- HIGH (80-100): One correct implementation, many wrong ones. Payment logic, crypto, auth, race conditions, data migrations, protocol compliance, correctness-critical algorithms. Getting it 95% right means it's wrong.
- MEDIUM (40-79): Several valid approaches but meaningful constraints. API design, database schema, error handling strategy, performance optimization with specific targets.
- LOW (0-39): Many acceptable outputs. UI styling, documentation, boilerplate scaffolding, config changes, dependency bumps, adding a standard CRUD endpoint. "Good enough" is the bar.
- Key question: If you gave this task to 10 competent developers independently, how similar would their implementations be? High similarity = high precision required.

## D. Cognitive Questions

19. cognitive_questions (0-100): Does the author ask GENUINE questions showing they are thinking?
NOT template headers ("### What changed?"). NOT rhetorical. NOT checklists.
Look for: "Should we also handle X?", "I'm not sure if this affects Y", "Does anyone know if Z is still used?", "Would it be better to...?", "What happens when...?"
These signal the author is cognitively engaged — they're thinking about edge cases, seeking feedback, or expressing uncertainty about their own work.
- 80-100: Multiple genuine questions showing deep engagement with the problem
- 40-79: One or two real questions mixed with template
- 10-39: Template questions only, or questions that are really statements

Score by asking: "If I handed this to a QA engineer who has never seen the codebase, could they write a test that fails before the change and passes after?"

TESTABILITY 10, acceptance_criteria 60:

→ Only testable assertion is "it still compiles." No behavioral test possible.

TESTABILITY 85, acceptance_criteria 40:
"Requests exceeding 100/minute per API key get 429 with Retry-After header set to seconds until reset."
→ Low acceptance_criteria (only one criterion) but HIGH testability (exact numbers, exact header, exact response code). One test, but it's completely automatable.

## Text to analyze:
Title: chore: add diagnostic logging for shard 38/40

## Summary

Add enhanced observability for Django Core test shards to enable root cause diagnosis for intermittent failures like shard 38/40.

### Changes

- **Environment diagnostics step**: Logs shard number, Python/ClickHouse versions, commit, branch, system resources (memory, disk), and Docker container status before tests run
- **Enhanced pytest output**: Added `-v` (verbose) and `--tb=short` flags to capture test names and stack traces
- **Extra test summary**: Added `-r fEsxX` flag to show detailed summary for failures, errors, skipped, xfailed, and xpassed tests

### Context

Django Core shard 38/40 experienced a sudden spike in failures on Jan 30, 2026:
- Historical baseline: 0% failure rate (353 consecutive successes over 7 days)
- Spike: 4 failures across different commits
- Root cause unknown due to insufficient logging

The failures occurred on unrelated commits (`d3cfd12`, `2ab1e35`), suggesting an infrastructure issue or flaky test rather than a code regression.

### What this enables

After 5-10 failures with enhanced logging, we can:
1. Identify specific test names that fail
2. See stack traces and error messages
3. Check environment state (memory, disk, containers) at time of failure
4. Determine if issue is timeout, assertion failure, or infrastructure problem

### Testing

This change only adds logging and doesn't affect test behavior. The pytest flags are well-established and used in other CI workflows.


---

> Created by [Mendral](https://mendral.com). Tag @mendral-app with feedback or questions.

> - Addresses [this insight](https://app.mendral.com/insights/01KG8NDAM204WTTXPKE69M0PKD)

author: dmarticus
error:
Command '['claude', '--model', 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001', '-p', 'You are analyzing a GitHub PR description or issue. Score it on TWO axes:
(A) SPEC QUALITY — how well does it describe what to build?
(B) HUMAN ENGAGEMENT — was a human genuinely thinking, or is this AI-generated boilerplate?

For every numeric field, score 0-100.

## A. Spec Quality Dimensions

1. outcome_clarity: Is the desired end state clear? Could two devs build the same thing?
2. error_states: Are error conditions, edge cases, failure modes described? 0 if none mentioned.
3. scope_boundaries: Clear what's IN and NOT in scope?
4. acceptance_criteria: Testable success conditions? Given/When/Then?
5. data_contracts: Data shapes, API contracts, schema changes? N/A if not data-related.
6. dependency_context: References to existing code, modules, APIs this touches?
7. behavioral_specificity: Concrete details (numbers, thresholds) vs vague ("should be fast")?
8. change_type: "feature"|"bugfix"|"refactor"|"dependency"|"docs"|"config"|"test"|"other"
9. spec_length_signal: "empty"|"minimal"|"short"|"medium"|"detailed"

## B. Human Engagement Signals

10. lived_experience: Author narrates what happened TO THEM? ("I ran into..." vs "The issue is...")
11. organizational_memory: References org knowledge an AI couldn't know? ("Per discussion with auth team")
12. uncertainty: Acknowledges unknowns? ("Should we also handle...?") AI never doubts itself.
13. negative_scope: Explicitly says what's NOT being done, with reasoning?
14. causal_reasoning: Explains WHY grounded in specific system context? (not "best practice")
15. genuine_edge_cases: System-specific edge cases, or generic CS concepts?
16. template_filler: (INVERTED: high = MORE template = bad) Is this just restating the diff?
17. overall_human_engagement: Overall: 80-100 clearly human, 50-79 mixed, 20-49 mostly AI, 0-19 pure AI.

## C. Task Precision

18. precision_required (0-100): How narrow is the correctness window for this task?
- HIGH (80-100): One correct implementation, many wrong ones. Payment logic, crypto, auth, race conditions, data migrations, protocol compliance, correctness-critical algorithms. Getting it 95% right means it's wrong.
- MEDIUM (40-79): Several valid approaches but meaningful constraints. API design, database schema, error handling strategy, performance optimization with specific targets.
- LOW (0-39): Many acceptable outputs. UI styling, documentation, boilerplate scaffolding, config changes, dependency bumps, adding a standard CRUD endpoint. "Good enough" is the bar.
- Key question: If you gave this task to 10 competent developers independently, how similar would their implementations be? High similarity = high precision required.

## D. Cognitive Questions

19. cognitive_questions (0-100): Does the author ask GENUINE questions showing they are thinking?
NOT template headers ("### What changed?"). NOT rhetorical. NOT checklists.
Look for: "Should we also handle X?", "I'm not sure if this affects Y", "Does anyone know if Z is still used?", "Would it be better to...?", "What happens when...?"
These signal the author is cognitively engaged — they're thinking about edge cases, seeking feedback, or expressing uncertainty about their own work.
- 80-100: Multiple genuine questions showing deep engagement with the problem
- 40-79: One or two real questions mixed with template
- 10-39: Template questions only, or questions that are really statements
- 0-9: No questions at all, or only boilerplate

## E. Testability of Success Criteria

20. testability (0-100): Could you write a FAILING test from this description alone, without reading the code?
This is NOT the same as acceptance_criteria. acceptance_criteria measures whether criteria exist. Testability measures whether you could AUTOMATE verification without reading the implementation.

Score by asking: "If I handed this to a QA engineer who has never seen the codebase, could they write a test that fails before the change and passes after?"

Examples to calibrate:

TESTABILITY 90, acceptance_criteria 85:
"POST /webhooks/stripe with valid HMAC-SHA256 signature returns 200. Invalid signature returns 401. Missing signature header returns 400."
→ QA engineer writes 3 HTTP tests immediately. No code reading needed.

TESTABILITY 20, acceptance_criteria 75:
"Given a user, when they log in, then they should be authenticated. Given invalid credentials, then login should fail gracefully."
→ Sounds testable but ISN'T. What endpoint? What response code? What does "gracefully" mean? 400? 401? 403? Redirect? QA engineer has to read the code or ask questions.

TESTABILITY 10, acceptance_criteria 60:
"This PR improves error handling across the auth module. Errors are now caught and reported consistently."
→ What errors? What does "consistently" mean? What's the observable difference? Cannot write any test.

TESTABILITY 5, acceptance_criteria 10:
"Refactors the middleware to use the new pattern. No functional changes."
→ Only testable assertion is "it still compiles." No behavioral test possible.

TESTABILITY 85, acceptance_criteria 40:
"Requests exceeding 100/minute per API key get 429 with Retry-After header set to seconds until reset."
→ Low acceptance_criteria (only one criterion) but HIGH testability (exact numbers, exact header, exact response code). One test, but it's completely automatable.

## Output
Return ONLY valid JSON, no markdown:
{
  "outcome_clarity": N, "error_states": N, "scope_boundaries": N,
  "acceptance_criteria": N, "data_contracts": N, "dependency_context": N,
  "behavioral_specificity": N,
  "change_type": "...", "spec_length_signal": "...",
  "lived_experience": N, "organizational_memory": N, "uncertainty": N,
  "negative_scope": N, "causal_reasoning": N, "genuine_edge_cases": N,
  "template_filler": N, "overall_human_engagement": N,
  "precision_required": N, "cognitive_questions": N, "testability": N,
  "evidence": {
    "lived_experience": "quote or none found",
    "organizational_memory": "quote or none found",
    "uncertainty": "quote or none found",
    "causal_reasoning": "quote or none found",
    "cognitive_questions": "quote the question or none found"
  },
  "classification": "human|ai_generated|mixed"
}

## Text to analyze:
Title: fix(cohorts):  self-heal stuck static cohorts and fix OOM in CH→PG sync

## Problem

When a user duplicates a large dynamic cohort as a static cohort, the `insert_cohort_from_query` Celery task populates ClickHouse and then syncs to Postgres. For large cohorts (1M+ people), the PG sync step in `insert_cohort_people_into_pg` loads **all** person UUIDs from ClickHouse into a Python list at once, which OOMs the worker. Since SIGKILL bypasses Python's `finally` block, `is_calculating` is never reset to `False`.

Worse, `reset_stuck_cohorts` explicitly excludes static cohorts, so stuck static cohorts are never auto-recovered — they stay stuck forever with `is_calculating=True`, `errors_calculating=0`, `last_calculation=null`, and no people visible in the UI despite the data existing in ClickHouse.

## Changes

**Fix the OOM** — `insert_cohort_people_into_pg` now uses cursor-based pagination (`WHERE person_id > last_id ORDER BY person_id LIMIT 10000`) via a new `CursorBatchIterator` instead of loading all rows into memory. Peak memory drops from O(n) to O(page_size).

**Make the task resumable** — `insert_cohort_from_query` now checks if ClickHouse already has rows for the cohort (from a previous partial run) and skips the CH insert to avoid duplicates from `generateUUIDv4()`. The PG sync is already resumable since `_insert_users_list_with_batching` deduplicates against existing rows each batch.

**Self-healing for stuck static cohorts** — `reset_stuck_cohorts` now also detects static cohorts with a `query` field that have been stuck for >1 hour. It resets `is_calculating`, bumps `errors_calculating`, and re-dispatches `insert_cohort_from_query`. Stops retrying after `MAX_ERRORS_CALCULATING` (20) failures.

**Move task to correct queue** — `insert_cohort_from_query` now runs on the `long_running` queue instead of the default `celery` queue.

**Add logging** — `insert_cohort_from_query` now logs at each step (task start, CH complete/skipped, PG complete, task finished) so stuck tasks are actually diagnosable in Grafana.

## How did you test this code?

- Added unit tests for `CursorBatchIterator` (basic pagination, empty, single batch, validation, uneven last batch)
- Updated `test_reset_stuck_cohorts` to verify static cohorts are now also reset
- Added `test_reset_stuck_static_cohorts_retriggers_query` to verify the self-healing re-dispatches the task
- Added `test_reset_stuck_static_cohorts_stops_after_max_errors` to verify it stops retrying after 20 failures
- Verified existing `test_insert_cohort_from_query_count_updated_on_exception` still passes with the new CH count check

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

N/A

## 🤖 LLM context

This PR was co-authored with an LLM agent while investigating a customer support issue where a static cohort created from a dynamic cohort was stuck in `is_calculating` state with 0 users visible despite data existing in ClickHouse.

title: fix(llma): fix trace-not-found when clicking view trace from sentiment tab
author: andrewm4894

evidence.lived_experience: Clicking "View trace" from the sentiment tab frequently shows "Trace not found."
evidence.organizational_memory: This matches the pattern used by the traces table, sessions scene, and all other trace link callers in LLM analytics
evidence.causal_reasoning: The raw ClickHouse timestamp (UTC, no timezone indicator) was passed directly. dayjs() treated it as local time and the backend's relative_date_parse treated it as project timezone, shifting the lookup window by the timezone offset. The sentiment query used max(ts) (latest event in trace) for the link, but the trace query's _map_results filter checks first_timestamp >= date_from. Since first_timestamp (earliest event) is always before max(ts), the trace was filtered out.

title: fix(cli,cymbal): create per-UUID ZIP for dSYM uploads
author: hpouillot

evidence.lived_experience: Tested by building the PostHog iOS example app and uploading dSYMs end-to-end: 1. First upload succeeded (3 symbol sets uploaded) 2. Made a source code string change, rebuilt, uploaded again — no `content_hash_mismatch` (1 new UUID uploaded, 2 skipped as already present with matching hash)
evidence.organizational_memory: PostHog iOS example app
evidence.uncertainty: Manual verification still needed: Cymbal symbolicating crash frames using the new format, Source bundling (`--include-source`) with per-UUID ZIPs
evidence.causal_reasoning: When a dSYM bundle contains multiple DWARF binaries (e.g. an Xcode debug dylib + app stub), the CLI zipped the entire bundle and shared that same blob across all UUIDs. On incremental rebuilds, stable UUIDs (like the app stub loader) got a new content hash whenever a sibling binary changed, causing `content_hash_mismatch` errors from the server.

- New automated test to test various cases

## Text to analyze:
Title: chore: wire worker_threads var to kafka-deduplicator

## Problem

The kafka-deduplicator's `worker_threads` config (default: 4) was declared but never wired into the tokio runtime. The `#[tokio::main]` macro defaults to CPU core count, making the config ineffective. Additionally, RocksDB batch read/write operations ran directly on the async runtime, risking tokio worker thread starvation and causing random liveness probe failures.

## Changes

- Replace `#[tokio::main]` with an explicit `tokio::runtime::Builder` that uses `config.worker_threads` to size the runtime
- Wrap `batch_read_timestamp_records` and `batch_write_timestamp_records` in `spawn_blocking` to keep RocksDB I/O off the async worker threads

12. uncertainty: Acknowledges unknowns? ("Should we also handle...?") AI never doubts itself.
13. negative_scope: Explicitly says what's NOT being done, with reasoning?
14. causal_reasoning: Explains WHY grounded in specific system context? (not "best practice")
15. genuine_edge_cases: System-specific edge cases, or generic CS concepts?
16. template_filler: (INVERTED: high = MORE template = bad) Is this just restating the diff?
17. overall_human_engagement: Overall: 80-100 clearly human, 50-79 mixed, 20-49 mostly AI, 0-19 pure AI.

## C. Task Precision

18. precision_required (0-100): How narrow is the correctness window for this task?
- HIGH (80-100): One correct implementation, many wrong ones. Payment logic, crypto, auth, race conditions, data migrations, protocol compliance, correctness-critical algorithms. Getting it 95% right means it's wrong.
- MEDIUM (40-79): Several valid approaches but meaningful constraints. API design, database schema, error handling strategy, performance optimization with specific targets.
- LOW (0-39): Many acceptable outputs. UI styling, documentation, boilerplate scaffolding, config changes, dependency bumps, adding a standard CRUD endpoint. "Good enough" is the bar.
- Key question: If you gave this task to 10 competent developers independently, how similar would their implementations be? High similarity = high precision required.

## D. Cognitive Questions

19. cognitive_questions (0-100): Does the author ask GENUINE questions showing they are thinking?
NOT template headers ("### What changed?"). NOT rhetorical. NOT checklists.
Look for: "Should we also handle X?", "I'm not sure if this affects Y", "Does anyone know if Z is still used?", "Would it be better to...?", "What happens when...?"
These signal the author is cognitively engaged — they're thinking about edge cases, seeking feedback, or expressing uncertainty about their own work.
- 80-100: Multiple genuine questions showing deep engagement with the problem
- 40-79: One or two real questions mixed with template
- 10-39: Template questions only, or questions that are really statements
- 0-9: No questions at all, or only boilerplate

## E. Testability of Success Criteria

20. testability (0-100): Could you write a FAILING test from this description alone, without reading the code?
This is NOT the same as acceptance_criteria. acceptance_criteria measures whether criteria exist. Testability measures whether you could AUTOMATE verification without reading the implementation.

Score by asking: "If I handed this to a QA engineer who has never seen the codebase, could they write a test that fails before the change and passes after?"

Examples to calibrate:

TESTABILITY 90, acceptance_criteria 85:
"POST /webhooks/stripe with valid HMAC-SHA256 signature returns 200. Invalid signature returns 401. Missing signature header returns 400."
→ QA engineer writes 3 HTTP tests immediately. No code reading needed.

TESTABILITY 20, acceptance_criteria 75:
"Given a user, when they log in, then they should be authenticated. Given invalid credentials, then login should fail gracefully."
→ Sounds testable but ISN'T. What endpoint? What response code? What does "gracefully" mean? 400? 401? 403? Redirect? QA engineer has to read the code or ask questions.

TESTABILITY 10, acceptance_criteria 60:
"This PR improves error handling across the auth module. Errors are now caught and reported consistently."
→ What errors? What does "consistently" mean? What's the observable difference? Cannot write any test.

TESTABILITY 5, acceptance_criteria 10:
"Refactors the middleware to use the new pattern. No functional changes."
→ Only testable assertion is "it still compiles." No behavioral test possible.

TESTABILITY 85, acceptance_criteria 40:
"Requests exceeding 100/minute per API key get 429 with Retry-After header set to seconds until reset."
→ Low acceptance_criteria (only one criterion) but HIGH testability (exact numbers, exact header, exact response code). One test, but it's completely automatable.

## Text to analyze:
Title: chore: simplify Stripe service catalog to free-only

## Problem

The Stripe Projects service catalog currently includes a `pay_as_you_go` paid plan and a `free` plan, with the `analytics` deployable as a component of both. When users run `stripe projects upgrade posthog/analytics`, Stripe collects payment and tries to call `update_service` - but the SPT billing integration isn't complete yet, so the upgrade fails.

## Changes

- Remove `free` and `pay_as_you_go` plan services from the catalog
- Simplify `analytics` deployable from a component (with free/paid options) to a simple free service
- Update `VALID_SERVICE_IDS` to only include `analytics`
- Update tests to reflect simplified catalog

The catalog now returns a single service:
```json
{
  "id": "analytics",
  "description": "PostHog - ...",
  "categories": ["analytics", "feature_flags", "ai"],
  "pricing": {"type": "free"},
  "kind": "deployable"
}
```

Plans will be re-added once SPT billing integration is complete (see #51946).

## How did you test this code?

- Updated unit tests in `test_services.py`, `test_resources.py`, `test_rotate_credentials.py`
- Verified syntax and linting pass

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Text to analyze:
Title: fix: change session_id type to str in QueryTags

## Problem

`POST /login` returns 500 when a request contains a malformed `session_id` query parameter. 

The `QueryTags` model types `session_id` as `Optional[uuid.UUID]` with `validate_assignment=True`, so any non-UUID string causes an unhandled `ValidationError` that crashes the request. This produced 134 Django errors over 48h ([details](https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0AMG1GR4VA/p1773838721887289?thread_ts=1773838699.486789&cid=C0AMG1GR4VA))

## Changes
 - Change `session_id` to `Optional[str]` in `QueryTags`, matching how the rest of the codebase treats session IDs

## How did you test this code?

Tests

## Publish to changelog?
No

## Docs update
No', '--output-format', 'text', '--max-turns', '1']' timed out after 60 seconds

Title: feat(rust): allow the timeout from the reusable workflow to pass linter

## Problem
The new smoke test workflow [failed here](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/23362067398) because GH Actions runtime checks don't like missing or duplicate cfg entries but our CI linting doesn't adjust for reusable workflows bringing in defaults like this.
<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Does this fix an issue? Uncomment the line below with the issue ID to automatically close it when merged -->
<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->

## Changes
* Fix it 🔧 
<!-- If there are frontend changes, please include screenshots. -->
<!-- If a reference design was involved, include a link to the relevant Figma frame! -->

## How did you test this code?
In CI of course. Will retest after this lands
<!-- Briefly describe the steps you took. -->

title: fix(cdp): Use truthy check for distinct_id in batch workflow person lookup and postHogCapture
author: dmarchuk

evidence.lived_experience: Batch workflow executions (e.g. sending emails to a matched audience) broke after #49658
evidence.organizational_memory: Batch invocations set distinct_id: '' (empty string) because there's no real event - they're triggered for a list of persons
evidence.causal_reasoning: The lazy loading code used ?? (nullish coalescing) which doesn't catch empty strings, only null/undefined. Without this, any workflow step using postHogCapture after the email action throws 'missing distinct_id'
